ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Penang Laksa Crispy Fish Head. FOODON_03315763 (fish dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for deep-fried fish head with Penang Laksa sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Malaysian fish dish preparation.

main_ingredients: fish head (large reef or freshwater fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Penang Laksa Crispy Fish Head is approximately 400 g, comprising a whole or half deep-fried fish head (around 300 g) plated with approximately 100 g of tangy Penang Laksa sauce poured over or served alongside.

nutrient_source.title: Deep-fried fish head with spiced tamarind sauce — estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data: deep-fried fish (item 05-C-0001) and Penang Laksa broth components
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single MyFCD entry exists for 'Penang Laksa Crispy Fish Head' as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining IMR MyFCD data for deep-fried fish (snapper/grouper head, skin-on) with Penang Laksa sauce components (tamarind broth, shrimp paste, aromatics). The fish head contributes the dominant protein, fat, and energy; the laksa sauce contributes sodium, carbohydrate, and fibre. Proportions estimated at approximately 75% fish head (deep-fried) and 25% sauce by weight. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA values for similar preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish and tamarind-based dishes — analogous estimate based on GI of fish-based dishes and tamarind broth; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Penang Laksa Crispy Fish Head. GI estimated at 38 based on analogous fish-dominant mixed dishes with tamarind-based sauces. Fish protein and fat significantly blunt glycemic response. Tamarind and vegetable components contribute modest carbohydrate. Analogous reference: mixed fish curry dishes (GI ~35–42) from University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 23.2) / 100 ≈ 8.8, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 5.8 × 4 = 23.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving) from shrimp paste, fish sauce, and seasoning in the Penang Laksa sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving or less, as a full 400 g serving provides approximately 1680 mg sodium — over 70% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg — and should request reduced shrimp paste and seasoning when ordering.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) and moderate carbohydrate content (~5.8 g per 100 g); high protein and fat content further blunts postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Penang Laksa Crispy Fish Head has a low GI of approximately 38 and provides only ~23 g of carbohydrate per serving, making it a relatively diabetes-friendly choice when consumed without rice or noodles, though the high sodium content warrants attention in individuals with diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; high sodium from shrimp paste and seasoning; however, fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried preparation elevates saturated fat and total fat intake, and the high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally, preferably with the sauce served on the side to reduce sodium exposure, and benefit from the omega-3 fatty acids naturally present in the fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Penang Laksa Crispy Fish Head is a Malaysian restaurant dish originating from Penang, combining two iconic culinary traditions: the deep-fried whole or half fish head (a staple of Chinese-Malaysian tze char cooking) and the bold, tangy flavours of Penang Laksa (Asam Laksa), a UNESCO-recognised heritage dish of Penang. The fish head — typically from a large reef fish such as red snapper or grouper — is marinated and deep-fried until the exterior is golden and crispy while the flesh remains moist and gelatinous near the bone. The Penang Laksa sauce is a tamarind-based (asam) spiced broth made with lemongrass, galangal, dried chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), torch ginger flower (bunga kantan), Vietnamese mint (daun kesum), and pineapple, producing a characteristically sour, spicy, and aromatic profile. The sauce is poured over or served alongside the crispy fish head, creating a textural contrast between the crunchy fried exterior and the intensely flavoured, slightly viscous sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~16.5 g per 100 g) from the fish, moderate in fat (~11.2 g per 100 g) due to deep-frying, and relatively low in carbohydrate (~5.8 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 38 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without rice or noodles. However, the sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per 400 g serving) due to shrimp paste and seasoning, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a popular sharing plate in Malaysian tze char restaurants and hawker-style eateries in Singapore, typically priced as a premium item due to the cost of the fish head.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Penang Laksa Crispy Fish Head as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data for deep-fried fish (snapper/grouper) and Penang Laksa sauce ingredients, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference data for similar fish-dominant dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from MyFCD is high quality and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data (deep-fried fish head ~75% by weight, Penang Laksa sauce ~25% by weight) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA. No single composite entry exists in any approved source for this specific dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this food category. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this food category. 4. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on mixed fish-dominant dishes with tamarind-based sauces from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 5. The cuisine_tags field in the input JSON includes 'Crab' and 'Beer', which appear to be restaurant-level tags for the establishment (George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer) rather than ingredients of this specific dish; these tags have not been incorporated into the food record. 6. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamins, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, and zinc_mg are estimated from MyFCD component data and should be treated as approximate. 7. The dish is served at George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer (Boat Quay, Singapore) at SGD 37.00 per portion, consistent with a premium shared fish head dish.
